summary: Simon Späti argues that the 'Open Data Stack' is a better framing than 'Modern Data Stack' because it emphasizes tools built on open source and open standards, which integrate better and reduce vendor lock-in. He presents four core tools as essential for any data-driven company: Airbyte for data integration, dbt for transformation, Metabase for analytics, and Dagster for orchestration. The post contrasts the old way of writing custom scripts for each data task with the modern approach of leveraging battle-tested open-source components. He emphasizes that this stack is modular and extensible, allowing teams to add specialized tools as needs grow.
key_points: The 'Open Data Stack' emphasizes open source and open standards over just being 'modern', enabling better tool integration and avoiding vendor lock-in
Four core tools form the foundation: Airbyte (integration), dbt (transformation), Metabase (visualization), and Dagster (orchestration)
The old approach of writing custom scripts for extraction, scheduling, and monitoring created unmaintainable, siloed solutions that broke without warning
dbt is called the 'king of SQL' because it forces software engineering best practices onto SQL transformations including testing, documentation, and version control
Dagster enforces functional data engineering principles—declarative, idempotent, type-checked functions that are testable in isolation
money_quotes: The Open Data Stack is a better term for the Modern Data Stack, but focusing on solutions built on open source and open standards covering the data engineering lifecycle.
With the open data stack, companies can reuse existing battle-tested solutions and build on top of them instead of having to reinvent the wheel.
Every data project starts with some SQL queries. The default should be to use dbt, which immediately forces you to use the best software engineering practices.
Write business logic with just a SQL select statement or a Python DataFrame that returns the dataset you need, and dbt takes care of materialization.
As data pipelines quickly grow in complexity and data teams grow in numbers, using methodologies that provide clarity isn't a luxury—it's a necessity.
The beauty of the data stack is that you can now add specific use cases with other tools and frameworks.
key_insight: The shift from custom data scripts to an open, composable data stack isn't just about using newer tools—it's about adopting battle-tested, interoperable components that enforce engineering best practices and eliminate the endless reinvention of foundational data infrastructure.
